title: Patient confidentiality: do not tell the spouse
vignette: A 28-year-old woman comes to the office for a regularly scheduled prenatal appointment. Her pregnancy has been going well and the patient says that she is thrilled to be having a baby after trying to become pregnant for several years. She and her husband experienced considerable strain in their marriage as they went through multiple unsuccessful fertility treatments. During the visit, the physician orders some blood work and performs a 1-hour glucose challenge test. The patient becomes apprehensive when this is mentioned and begins to ask questions about the purpose of the testing. When asked about her concern, she confides that the fetus is not her husband's child. Her husband is also the physician's patient, and she asks the physician not to tell him about this because "it will ruin everything." Which of the following is the most appropriate response by the physician?

clues.text: the fetus is not her husband's child
clues.note: This is sensitive, potentially stigmatizing personal information disclosed in confidence. Physicians are obligated to protect such private health information unless a specific exception to confidentiality applies.

clues.text: Her husband is also the physician's patient
clues.note: The husband being a patient creates an apparent conflict - but confidentiality is owed to EACH patient individually. The physician cannot share one patient's private information with another without consent.

clues.text: she asks the physician not to tell him
clues.note: The patient explicitly requests confidentiality. Absent an exception (abuse, certain reportable diseases, imminent harm), this request must be respected; the correct response simply honors it without judgment.

whatMatters: Strip the story to: a patient shares sensitive reproductive information and asks the physician not to tell her husband (who is also a patient). The physician's duty is to protect HER confidentiality, and no exception applies (no abuse, reportable disease, or imminent harm). The best response simply RESPECTS her confidentiality without judgment or pressure: 'I will not tell your husband.' Answer E.
strategy: For confidentiality dilemmas, default to PROTECTING the patient's private information unless a specific exception applies (suspected abuse, certain communicable diseases, gunshot/knife wounds, imminent threat of serious harm). Then pick the response that is neutral and nonjudgmental - avoid options that lecture, pressure, or moralize. The clean 'I will keep your confidence' answer wins.

options.text: "I am obligated to tell him as he is my patient and it is in his best interest to know."
"I will not inform your husband, but please consider telling him the truth."
"I will not tell him, but I am obligated to inform the biological father."
"I will not tell him, but keeping this from your husband will have many negative effects."

eliminations.label: breaks confidentiality / imposes a value
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: a claim that the husband being a patient obligates disclosure 'in his best interest.' ❌ WHY IT FAILS: confidentiality is owed to each patient individually; her private information cannot be shared without her consent, and 'his best interest' imposes the physician's value judgment. ✅ Correct if: a true exception applied (eg, a reportable communicable disease threatening him).

eliminations.label: keeps confidence but is directive
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: keeps confidence but URGES her to tell him. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: although it protects confidentiality, it is directive/judgmental, pushing her toward an action she may not be ready for. ✅ Correct if: she had asked for advice about whether to disclose.

eliminations.label: no duty to inform the biological father
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: keeps confidence from the husband but claims a duty to inform the biological father. ❌ WHY IT FAILS: the physician has no legal or ethical obligation to notify the biological father. ✅ Correct if: never in this scenario.

eliminations.label: keeps confidence but pressures/judges
eliminations.detail: WHAT IT IS: keeps confidence but warns of 'many negative effects.' ❌ WHY IT FAILS: it is judgmental and pressures her, rather than neutrally respecting her decision. ✅ Correct if: she requested counseling about consequences.

reasoning: Sensitive private information disclosed in confidence
Patient explicitly requests confidentiality; no exception applies
Duty is to respect her confidentiality, neutrally and nonjudgmentally
Best response: 'I will not tell your husband' -> answer E
mechanism: Physicians are ethically obligated to respect patient autonomy and protect confidentiality, keeping personal health information private unless the patient consents to disclosure. This is especially important for stigmatizing issues (reproductive, sexual, substance-use, mental-health). Confidentiality has defined exceptions: suspected child/elder/disabled-person abuse, knife/gunshot wounds, certain reportable communicable diseases, and a credible threat of serious harm to self or others. None apply here, and the husband also being a patient does not override her confidentiality. The most appropriate response simply and nonjudgmentally honors her request.

difficulty.label: 📚 Pure knowledge gap
difficulty.message: First Aid Behavioral science/Ethics > patient confidentiality and its exceptions; autonomy and nonjudgmental communication. Cross-ref duty to warn (Tarasoff).

difficulty.label: 🧭 Understanding the question
difficulty.message: Two checks: (1) does any exception apply? (No abuse, reportable disease, or imminent harm.) (2) Which response is neutral and non-directive? Choose the plainest 'I will keep your confidence.'

difficulty.label: 🔤 Vocabulary
difficulty.message: Unsure on confidentiality, autonomy, exceptions to confidentiality, duty to warn, nonjudgmental/non-directive communication? Tell me which and I'll explain it.

difficulty.label: 🧲 Pulled by a distractor
difficulty.message: Options B and D are traps because they DO keep the secret - but they tack on a lecture or pressure. The exam rewards the neutral, respectful answer (E), not the moralizing one.

difficulty.label: ✍️ Something else
difficulty.message: Bank the rule: protect confidentiality by default; only break it for abuse, reportable disease, gunshot/knife wounds, or imminent serious harm.

keywords.front: Patient asks physician not to tell spouse private info

keywords.back: Abuse (child/elder/disabled), gunshot/knife wounds, reportable communicable disease, imminent harm to self/others

keywords.front: Best communication style in ethics answers

keywords.back: Still owe each patient separate confidentiality
Imminent threat of serious harm to an identifiable person

firstAid.detail: Behavioral science/Ethics > confidentiality and its exceptions; respect for autonomy.
firstAid.location: Behavioral science/Ethics > confidentiality and its exceptions; respect for autonomy.

firstAid.detail: Ethics > nonjudgmental, non-directive responses; duty to warn vs confidentiality.
firstAid.location: Ethics > nonjudgmental, non-directive responses; duty to warn vs confidentiality.

memoryHook: Her secret, her call. No abuse, no reportable disease, no threat = keep confidentiality, plainly and without a lecture.
